source_paper_title: Quantifying Distributional Model Risk in Marginal Problems via Optimal Transport
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Fan_2025, title={Quantifying Distributional Model Risk in Marginal Problems via Optimal Transport}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0557}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0557}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Fan, Yanqin and Park, Hyeonseok and Xu, Gaoqian}, year={2025}, month=aug }
context:
Page 35, Section 8 (Concluding Remarks).

Many open questions remain including the structure of optimizers of W-DMR for both non-overlapping and overlapping marginals, efficient numerical algorithms, and estimation and inference in each motivating example.
title: Characterize optimizers of Wasserstein distributional model risk in marginal problems
background_latex:
Let \((S_1,d_{S_1})\) and \((S_2,d_{S_2})\) be Polish metric spaces, and let \(\mu_1\in\mathcal P(S_1)\), \(\mu_2\in\mathcal P(S_2)\) be given Borel probability measures (reference marginals). Let \(V:=S_1\times S_2\), and for any \(\gamma\in\mathcal P(V)\) write \(\gamma_1\) and \(\gamma_2\) for its marginals on \(S_1\) and \(S_2\), respectively.

Fix measurable cost functions \(c_1:S_1\times S_1\to \mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) and \(c_2:S_2\times S_2\to \mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) such that \(c_\ell(x,y)=0\) iff \(x=y\) (and possibly asymmetric and allowed to take value \(\infty\)). For \(\ell\in\{1,2\}\), define the optimal transport cost
\[
K_\ell(\mu_\ell,\nu_\ell):=\inf_{\pi\in\Pi(\mu_\ell,\nu_\ell)} \int c_\ell\,d\pi,
\]
where \(\Pi(\mu_\ell,\nu_\ell)\) denotes the set of couplings of \(\mu_\ell\) and \(\nu_\ell\).

Given a radius vector \(\delta=(\delta_1,\delta_2)\in\mathbb R_+^2\), define the marginal Wasserstein uncertainty set
\[
\Sigma_D(\delta):=\Bigl\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(V): K_1(\mu_1,\gamma_1)\le \delta_1,\; K_2(\mu_2,\gamma_2)\le \delta_2\Bigr\}.
\]
Let \(g:V\to\mathbb R\) be a measurable objective function such that \(\int g\,d\gamma\) is well-defined (e.g., bounded below on at least one feasible coupling) for relevant \(\gamma\).

The Wasserstein distributional model risk in this marginal setting is the value
\[
I_D(\delta):=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma_D(\delta)} \int g\,d\gamma.
\]
An optimizer (when it exists) is a measure \(\gamma^\star\in\Sigma_D(\delta)\) achieving this supremum, i.e., \(\int g\,d\gamma^\star = I_D(\delta)\). (Analogous definitions apply to the paper's overlapping-marginals formulation on \(S=Y_1\times Y_2\times X\), with projections \(\gamma_{13},\gamma_{23}\) constrained in optimal-transport balls around given reference measures \(\mu_{13},\mu_{23}\).)
problem_definition_latex:
Describe the structure of optimizers \(\gamma^\star\) that attain
\[
I_D(\delta)=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma_D(\delta)} \int g\,d\gamma
\]
in the Wasserstein distributional model risk problem with marginal (non-overlapping) constraints, and analogously describe the structure of optimizers in the overlapping-marginals formulation
\[
I(\delta)=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma(\delta)} \int f\,d\gamma,
\]
where \(\Sigma(\delta)=\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(Y_1\times Y_2\times X): K_1(\mu_{13},\gamma_{13})\le\delta_1,\;K_2(\mu_{23},\gamma_{23})\le\delta_2\}\).
In particular, determine qualitative/structural properties (e.g., existence and form of extremal couplings, support/graphical structure, conditional independence/monotonicity-type features, sparsity, or representation in terms of optimal transport maps/plans) that such maximizers must satisfy as functions of \((g,c_1,c_2,\mu_1,\mu_2,\delta)\) (and analogously \((f,\mu_{13},\mu_{23})\) in the overlapping case).
latex:

open_problem_quote: Many open questions remain including the structure of optimizers of W-DMR for both non-overlapping and overlapping marginals, efficient numerical algorithms, and estimation and inference in each motivating example.
subject_classification: Distributionally robust optimization and optimal transport in marginal problems
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a general structural characterization of optimizers across broad classes of objectives, costs, and reference marginals, not a falsifiable finite statement. Automated instance search could illustrate behaviors in specific discrete examples but would not directly resolve the general structural problem.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no clear parametrized candidate solution concept with an automatic verifier for correctness of a claimed structural theorem. While code could compute optimizers numerically for instances, it would not provide a reliable fitness signal toward proving a general characterization.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
